This study furnishes insight into investors, portfolio managers and policy analysts. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: CDS; Digital assets; Dynamic connectedness; ETFs; Portfolio diversification

Authors: Han J.; Lu T.; Xu Y.; Zhang C.
Title: Watch your Wallet closely with online microloans: a two-stage model for delinquency and default risk management
Abstract: Online microloans are a growing financial service. Therefore, resolving the question of how to manage the dynamics of an individual borrower’s risk is crucial. By applying an installment-level borrower behavior dataset, we developed a comprehensive two-stage model for assessing borrowers’ delinquency and default risk for each installment of a loan. We also discovered new risk antecedents such as a borrower’s previous repayment behavior and installment due date factors. This study uncovered three segments of borrowers and their corresponding risk levels. Furthermore, the study evaluated the economic benefit of debt collection. The study findings provide a comprehensive model for evaluating borrowers’ delinquency and risk at the individual and segment levels over time. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2023.
Author Keywords: Debt collection; Default; Delinquency; Microloan; Online microlending

Authors: Diao W.; Zhang Y.
Title: The value of anonymized data option in integrating third-party data
Abstract: Balancing data utilization and privacy protection is crucial for fostering the sustainable growth of the digital economy. This study examines the role of user anonymized data authorization in the context of monopoly platforms' third-party data integration decisions. The findings reveal that the platform’s decision to integrate third-party data is influenced by the commission rate, the effectiveness of anonymized data in protecting privacy, and the volume of third-party data available. When the platform has amassed a substantial amount of user data and anonymized data provides robust privacy protection, data integration can create a win–win scenario, benefiting both platform profits and user welfare. While data integration leads to a reduction in transaction prices, it enhances market share through precise personalized recommendation services, achieving profit growth despite operating on “thin profit margins.” Users’ decisions to authorize anonymized data are shaped by a trade-off between the negative utility of personalized services and the positive utility of privacy protection. Notably, users are less likely to authorize anonymized data when the platform implementing data integration already possesses a significant volume of user data. For enterprise practice, platforms should calibrate integration scale based on their first-party data coverage and anonymization strength, conducting small-scale, rigorously vetted anonymized data pilots in high-coverage markets, bolstering first-party data collection where compliance costs are high, and employing tiered opt-in incentives to encourage raw data sharing; meanwhile, policymakers should establish clear, graduated anonymization standards and subsidize open-source privacy toolkits to lower compliance burdens and build user trust. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Anonymized data; Data integration; Data privacy; Platform economy

Authors: Cheng C.; Yue Z.
Title: A non-uniform human resource equalization recommendation algorithm considering the needs of business users
Abstract: Human resources data usually contains diversified information involving different positions, industries, regions, and other aspects. It is uneven and complex, such as market fluctuations, and cannot extract adequate information, making human resources recommendations’ accuracy low. To improve recruitment efficiency, a non-uniform human resource balanced recommendation algorithm is proposed considering the needs of enterprise users. Obtain the enterprise user demand preference data according to the recruitment websites of domestic and foreign enterprises, clean and clear the redundant data in the collected data through the kettle tool, and form a dataset. Using the pre-processed enterprise user demand preference data, the collaborative filtering recommendation algorithm and the bipartite graph recommendation algorithm are effectively combined, and a bipartite graph recommendation algorithm showing preferences is proposed. Through this algorithm, the initial value of job seekers and the human resource allocation coefficient in the bipartite graph recommendation algorithm is adjusted to realize the uneven recommendation of human resources for enterprise users. Through experimental verification, it is known that this algorithm can achieve human resources recommendation, the recommendation results meet the needs of enterprise users, the recommendation effect is better, the generalization ability is strong, and it can more comprehensively solve the problem of enterprise talent supply. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Balanced recommendation algorithms; Bipartite graph recommendation; Considering business user needs; Data collection; Non-uniform human resources

Authors: Kang W.; Fu X.; Xiao S.
Title: Priority-based approach for seller-buyer matching in E-commerce under uncertain preferences
Abstract: The rapid evolution of electronic commerce (e-commerce) has reshaped traditional industries and marketing. In this context, this study addresses the challenge of seller-buyer matching congestion on e-commerce platforms, particularly in used car trading. To model the uncertainty and heterogeneity in user preferences, this study employs interval numbers and prospect theory to calculate the gains and losses of buyers and sellers, thereby capturing both ambiguous expectations and psychological behaviors. A priority-based matching framework is then proposed to adjust satisfaction scores according to platform-defined user priorities and individual preferences. This adjustment allows the platform to promote high-priority participants and reduce waiting times without compromising overall performance. A stable matching model is formulated to maximize bilateral satisfaction while ensuring implementable outcomes simultaneously. A case study and extensive simulations can prove the proposed method is effective in improving satisfaction, reducing average waiting times, and maintaining matching stability. The study contributes to existing literature by combining uncertainty preferences with priority-based matching to enhance theoretical understanding and practical performance of two-sided matching in congested environments. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: E-commerce; Prioritizing; Prospect theory; Stable matching; Uncertain preferences

Authors: Bambauer-Sachse S.
Title: Do consumers prefer lower or higher numbers of customizable product attributes in an e-commerce context?
Abstract: The studies presented in this paper examine the effects of different levels of online product customization on consumers’ product evaluations and purchase likelihood and sheds light on the different processes underlying these consumer reactions. Moreover, the paper analyzes whether consumer reactions to comparatively high levels of customization differ when additional standard options are available. Five survey-based studies, four with simulated customization and one with real customization, address these research questions. The results show that consumer reactions to a higher number of customizable product attributes are less positive when only a customization tool is available. However, if standard fallback options are available, customization of a comparatively high number of customizable attributes can generate positive product evaluations and a high purchase likelihood. Therefore, electronic commerce managers can be recommended to offer additional standard options if they implement a customization tool with a relatively high number of customizable product attributes. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Confusion; Effort; Preference fit; Pride; Product customization

Authors: Sánchez-Paniagua M.; Fidalgo E.; Alegre E.; Jáñez-Martino F.
Title: Fraud detection in e-commerce: a comparative analysis of features to enhance machine learning models
Abstract: In recent years, e-commerce has experienced growth in sales, brands and customers. Unfortunately, cybercriminals have taken advantage of this by creating fraudulent websites to scam customers. The large amount of new e-commerce websites outnumbers the manual reporting capabilities, exposing users to these attacks. In this work, we used machine learning techniques to identify possible fraudulent online stores. To achieve this, we created ELFW-2031 (E-commerce Legitimate Fraudulent Websites), an updated dataset of manually verified legitimate and fraudulent e-commerce websites and a comprehensive set of resources for researchers to compare their methods. We released this dataset for public use to overcome the lack of a comprehensive corpus of this type of websites. We also designed a novel set of 50 features using six different resources obtained from the website content and external services. We used these new features to train and test two models: (i) a model with all available resources focused on improving accuracy and (ii) a model focused on scalability independent of external services. The proposed models achieve F1 scores of 96.88% and 96.53% respectively using XGBoost. Finally, we evaluated the performance of the proposed features, showing that novel features from social media and the technology analysis were the most valuable ones. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: E-commerce; Feature engineering; Fraud detection; Machine learning

Authors: Akter M.; Kummer T.-F.; Green P.
Title: How issue-relevant information and peripheral cues shape investment decisions in equity crowdfunding
Abstract: Equity crowdfunding has received growing popularity as an innovative financing model. However, despite its growth, the average success rate of campaigns remains low. The digital nature of this model introduces unique challenges, including high information asymmetry, limited interaction and the absence of intermediaries. Previous studies mostly focus on the rational, direct impacts of information signals, few explore both rational and heuristic aspects and their interplay in influencing campaign outcomes. This research, using the elaboration likelihood model, analyses data from 285 equity crowdfunding campaigns to examine the direct and interactive influence of issue-relevant information and peripheral cues on campaign success. The findings demonstrate the significance of both issue-relevant information (i.e., team quality and innovativeness) and peripheral cues (i.e., readability and assurance) on equity crowdfunding success. In particular, the results reveal new insights into the interactions between these factors. The study offers implications for crowdfunding platforms and entrepreneurs, directing strategies to effectively manage information to enhance equity crowdfunding outcomes. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Elaboration likelihood model; Equity crowdfunding; Information; Online investors; Peripheral cues

Authors: Cui Y.
Title: Influence of E-commerce on rural residents’ income: a multi-time difference-in-differences model
Abstract: This study seeks to clarify whether e-commerce increases rural incomes in China and, if so, whether this impact is heterogeneous. Based on panel data of 1,546 county-level units from 2012 to 2019, this study investigates e-commerce’s impact on rural residents’ incomes using a multi-time difference-in-differences model. Results show that, from a national perspective, e-commerce has improved rural residents’ income, and employment and entrepreneurship are the main mechanisms. Additionally, e-commerce’s income-increasing effect heterogeneous, with the strongest positive influence on rural resident income in the central and northeast regions and the weakest in the western region. At the same time, initial income and human capital levels also lead to a difference in the impact of e-commerce on rural residents’ income. Based on this, the paper proposes increasing support for rural e-commerce; developing local characteristic industries, improving the competitiveness of local rural e-commerce products, and cultivating e-commerce talents. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: China; E-commerce; Employment and entrepreneurship; Income of rural residents

Authors: Waldmann F.; Nápoles G.; Salgueiro Y.
Title: Prediction of cart abandonment using imbalanced clickstream data in online shopping
Abstract: Numerous AI-based solutions have been developed to predict online shopping behavior and cart abandonment, ranging from statistical and sequential data mining approaches to deep learning models. However, this task remains challenging due to the inherent class imbalance of online shopping data and the lack of transparency of most successful classifiers. In this paper, we investigate both issues in the context of cart abandonment prediction using imbalanced clickstream data in online shopping. As a first contribution, we study the effectiveness of addressing class imbalance via thresholding compared to other undersampling and oversampling in the context of clickstream data. Therefore the thresholding method is investigated that balances the decisions generated by deep neural networks by adjusting the probability threshold through binary search. The advantage of this strategy is that it neither discards any relevant data samples nor introduces synthetic ones. As a second contribution, we study the contribution of sequential and static features to the model’s performance and investigate the extent to which oversampling techniques induce noise in the interpretability results. The numerical simulations show that our balancing strategy is slightly superior to undersampling and oversampling approaches without inducing a bias towards any of the decision classes. Moreover, we noticed that integrating static features alongside sequential data further boosts the performance of the recurrent neural network models, aligning with the results of previous research. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Cart abandonment; Class imbalance; Clickstream data; Deep learning; Explainable AI

Authors: Banerjee S.; Sen A.; Zahay D.
Title: The effect of in-store electronic word of mouth on local competitor spillovers in the quick service restaurant industry
Abstract: The quick service restaurant (QSR) industry operates in a highly competitive environment with close proximity, facilitating switching behavior. Therefore, QSRs have experimented with menus, developed personalized experiences, and pioneered new methods of ordering and delivery to improve sales and revenue. A less used method by QSRs is managing social media resources, particularly electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM), to obtain competitive advantage. This paper explores the effect of in-store engagement in the form of geolocated social media messages containing product-brand mentions in QSRs by combining data from multiple digital data sources. This research supports the ideas that parasocial (not face-to-face) interactions such as likes, retweets, and replies fully mediate the effect of in-store engagement on local competitor visits over the next 30 days, and that the direction of this effect can vary with competitor density in the location. This research suggests how QSRs can use social media to improve local market positions. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2023.
Author Keywords: Competitor spillover; Electronic word of mouth; eWOM; Social media; Twitter

Authors: Darwish S.M.; Salama A.I.; Elzoghabi A.A.; El-Shoafy N.A.
Title: An intelligent memetic approach to detect online fraud for distributed fintech environments
Abstract: Detecting fraudulent trading activities in the FinTech sector presents significant challenges due to the highly dynamic nature of financial markets and the rapid adaptation strategies employed by fraudsters. Conventional machine learning techniques, especially when trained on imbalanced datasets, often exhibit classification bias toward the majority class (legitimate transactions), reducing their ability to identify fraudulent behaviors. To address this limitation, we introduce a hybrid memetic-ABC framework that combines Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) sampling for global exploration with memetic local refinement to generate high-quality synthetic minority-class samples guided by feature importance. ABC ensures broad coverage of potential fraudulent patterns, while the memetic refinement fine-tunes synthetic samples and feature subsets to enhance discriminative power, capturing subtle anomalies in transaction amounts, locations, and timing. The framework further incorporates an anomaly detection module that constructs behavioral profiles for traders using historical transaction data, clustering them based on similar trading patterns. This enables identification of key indicators of potential fraud, such as sudden volume fluctuations, irregular transaction timings, and deviations from behavioral norms. For real-time monitoring and scalable deployment in decentralized environments, the system is extended into a Distributed Intrusion Detection System (DIDS) architecture, simultaneously detecting transactional anomalies and network-level threats, including DDoS attacks and unauthorized access attempts. By correlating anomalous trading patterns with DIDS-generated alerts, the approach achieves enhanced detection accuracy and robustness against complex fraud scenarios. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms state-of-the-art techniques, achieving approximately 10% improvement in accuracy. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Distributed anomaly detection system; E-commerce fraud detection; FinTech security analytics; Imbalanced financial transaction data; Swarm intelligence-based sampling

Authors: Lin H.; Pan Q.; Zheng X.; Jin G.
Title: A quasi-experimental study on the mechanism of product certification services in e-commerce trading platforms: empirical evidence derived from high-end sneakers on a prominent e-commerce platform
Abstract: Certification holds significant economic value in business transactions, serving as a unique signal and quality assurance amidst information asymmetry between buyers and sellers. To effectively address issues of fake goods and quality control and to continuously optimise the shopping experience for buyers, numerous e-commerce platforms are either exploring or have already implemented certification services for genuine products. Despite the widespread adoption of certification services by e-commerce platforms as a mechanism to address information asymmetry, existing research has examined the effectiveness of these services in mitigating information asymmetry in e-commerce transactions using both qualitative and quantitative methods, and has uniformly concluded that certification services play a definitive role. However, the existing research has not yet clearly elucidated the reasons why certification services can alleviate information asymmetry on e-commerce platforms, the mechanisms through which certification services impact sellers’ sales, or the influence of sellers’ initial sales volumes on the effectiveness of the certification mechanisms. This paper, consequently, builds upon the theories of information asymmetry, signaling theory, and quality assurance mechanisms. Leveraging the research opportunities afforded by quasi-natural experiments conducted on e-commerce platforms, it employs transaction data for high-end sneakers sourced from Platform E to delve into the specific role of certification services. The empirical results indicate that after sellers introduce certification services, their average weekly sales increase by 9.5%, with the underlying mechanism being that high-quality certification services bolster buyer willingness. Further testing reveals that the effect of certification services on increasing sales is more pronounced for smaller sellers. This study clearly identifies the commercial value of certification services, providing important insights for leveraging product certification services to enhance standardized management on e-commerce platforms, reshape the transaction value chain, and ultimately unlock greater market potential and value. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Certification services; E-commerce transactions; High-end sneakers; Value of certification services

Authors: Zheng S.; Wu Y.; Hu J.; Yin N.
Title: The impact of institutional investors on enterprise innovation quality: based on exit threat mechanism and corporate decision-making
Abstract: Innovation is an endogenous driving force of high-quality economic development and structural transformation in China. Using major institutional investors, this study explores the impact of exit threat mechanisms and corporate decision-making on enterprise innovation output quality. Empirical results show that the exit threat of institutional investors reduces information asymmetry in innovation activities, alleviating the short-sighted or self-interest behaviours of internal decision-makers in the innovation process to improve the enterprise innovation quality. Meanwhile, the threat of institutional investors withdrawing from innovation activities enhances the efficiency of innovation resource utilisation and capabilities and promotes high-quality innovation by alleviating agency problems in innovation activities. Robustness tests are conducted by using alternative explanatory and dependent variables, and the results remain robust. Instrumental variable, Heckman tests, and fisrt-difference model tests are conducted to examined the endogeneity generated by the omitted variables, self-selection and reciprocal causal problems, and the conclusion still holds. Results indicate that in emerging countries such as China, improving diversified equity structures and strengthening the regulatory role of major external shareholders help promote the quality development of enterprise innovation, enhance China’s technological innovation capabilities, and help build an innovative country. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Corporate governance; Exit threats; Innovation quality; Institutional investors

Authors: Zhang L.; Guo Y.
Title: Multimarket contact and unethical market behavior: empirical evidence from a micro-survey of internet enterprises
Abstract: While existing literature primarily focuses on the impact of multimarket contact on inter-enterprise competition, it lacks analysis on the variation of firm market behavior under multimarket contact. This paper utilizes micro-survey data from Internet enterprises to empirically examine whether multimarket contact induces unethical behaviors such as “familiarity exploitation” and “price discrimination” among Internet enterprises. Based on data from a microsurvey of large Chinese Internet enterprises, the findings reveal that increased multimarket contact significantly increases unethical behavior. Furthermore, market size and resource similarity reinforce the impact of multimarket contact on unethical behavior. This conclusion holds true after robustness checks. Notably, the strategy of unethical market behavior exhibits action contagion among direct competitors, evolving into a collective immorality and the formation of “unspoken rules”. Finally, the paper presents relevant policy recommendations. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Internet enterprises; Market competition; Multimarket contact; Unethical market behavior

Authors: Qin J.; Zhao M.; Ren J.; Li B.
Title: Pricing strategy for C2C sharing platform considering the effect of WOM on purchase decision
Abstract: Consumers often view online reviews as trustworthy sources of information in Consumer-to-Consumer (C2C) sharing economy, these online reviews create a word-of-mouth (WOM). This paper focuses on four pricing strategies: fixed pricing based on registration fee, dynamic pricing based on registration fee, fixed pricing based on transaction fee and dynamic pricing based on transaction fee, to judge the impact of WOM. Consider a C2C sharing economy market composed of consumers, suppliers, and a platform, construct a game theory model, and obtain the equilibrium solution using backward induction. We find that a moderate WOM always makes the fixed pricing strategy attract more demand. However, the dynamic pricing strategy consistently yields higher profit. Furthermore, if total demand and profit are pursued by the platform at the same time, WOM production makes the dominant strategy shift to fixed pricing strategy based on transaction fee and dynamic pricing strategy based on transaction fee. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2024.
Author Keywords: C2C sharing economy; Platform economy; Pricing strategy; Two-sided market; WOM effect

Authors: Sun Y.
Title: Versioning strategies for online dating platforms
Abstract: This study examined information service versioning strategies for online dating platforms. Several research questions regarding versioning have been proposed. First, is it profitable for a platform to offer multiple versions of its service? If yes, how many versions should be offered? If gender-based price discrimination is infeasible, how does that affect the versioning strategies? We found that, first, compared to offering a single version, it is more profitable for a platform to provide multiple versions of its services. Second, the optimal number of versions for the platform to provide is two: basic and premium. Third, the maximal profits obtained by the platform increase with the quality of the premium version but change ambiguously with the quality of the basic version. Finally, a ban on gender-based price discrimination does not fundamentally affect versioning strategies. The user welfare of the two groups (male and female users) is affected in opposite directions, but the effect is weakened by cross-group network effects. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2023.
Author Keywords: Cross-group network effects (CGNEs); Dating service; Multi-sided platforms (MSPs); Versioning

Authors: Kavusi H.; Maghooli K.; Haghipour S.
Title: A novel algorithm to model the neuromuscular system from the eye to fingers to authenticate individuals through a typing process
Abstract: The extensive use of computers has necessitated a new paradigm, in which computers are not only the major channel for dealing with day-to-day financial, industrial, and individual duties, but also the need to establish effective user identification for authentication reasons. Based on this fact, behavioral biometrics, such as typing, can be used for authentication to be subtle, unlike most biometrics. In this paper, to verify the identity, an adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS) is employed to model musculoskeletal system from the eye to the fingers in the typing process, as well as to model the control process of typing behavior. Model predictive control (MPC) is used to model the control process in order to get the best results. The improved distance evaluation (IDE) feature selection technique is utilized to minimize feature dimensions, and data fusion is conducted at the feature level. Besides, the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier is applied to authenticate selected features. Moreover, this algorithm is tested on a dataset of 35 users, providing accuracy with an Arithmetic mean of 99.65. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2022.
Author Keywords: Anfis; Authentication; Biometric; IDE; MPC; SVM

Authors: Lehmann-Zschunke N.; Ampler N.
Title: Which keyword design increases the click and purchase probability? An empirical analysis of content-related, formal and competition-related design criteria
Abstract: In online retail, the majority of consumers use a search engine before making a purchase decision. Search engine advertising is important for the commercial use of a search engine. This study analyzes the influence of content-related, formal and competition-related criteria for the keyword design on click and purchase probability. Using logistic regression, we analyze the click and purchase probabilities of various keywords based on an extensive data set from an e-commerce retailer. The empirical study shows that keyword specificity and quality positively impact click and purchase probability, while the number of product details and the distance of a bit have a negative effect. Two of the three formal design criteria show little to no significant influence on the target variables. The results provide advertisers with insights into how they should design their keywords. They should pay particular attention to the content-related keyword design criteria and the quality of the keyword. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Click probability; Design criteria; Keyword design; Purchase probability; Search engine advertising

Authors: Chen K.; Li Q.; Goh M.
Title: Recycling or selling? Strategic entry decisions of the primary platform in the secondhand market
Abstract: Amid the growing prominence of the platform economy, the strategic choices of functionally differentiated platforms in the secondhand market have become a critical issue. This study explores the partnership between a primary platform (E1) and a secondhand recycling or sales platform (E2), analyzing how different modes impact their decision-making and profitability. To explore whether E1 participates in E2’s recycling or secondhand sales mode, we first study the case of non-participation mode as a benchmark. The results shows that when the refurbishment cost is high, E1 gains the highest profit by engaging in secondhand sale, while E2 benefits more from engaging in recycling. Under certain conditions, a non-participation scenario may exert an environmental impact, leading to positive effects on consumer surplus and social welfare. In contrast, both engagement in recycling and engagement in secondhand sales can lead to mutually beneficial outcomes, particularly under all-resale and no-resale strategies. In our extended analysis, we explore the impact of platform power on the profit of each member and find that it generally benefits E2. In addition, when E1 pursues a dual objective of both profitability and consumer surplus, consumer surplus always benefits, and E1 benefits from consistent profitability under certain conditions. Finally, when there is uncertainty in the quantity of secondhand products, we find that as demand uncertainty increases, the expected profit of both platforms follows an upward (downward) trend in all-resale and no-resale (partial-resale) strategies. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Platform closed-loop supply chain; Recycling mode; Resale strategy; Secondhand market; Selling mode

Authors: Liu Y.; Ma W.
Title: Ecological strategies for rural industrial and green digital poverty alleviation: an empirical analysis in China
Abstract: The study examines environmentally friendly strategie’s spatial patterns and influences on rural industrial green digital poverty alleviation from 2014 to 2022. The study uses spatial econometric models to investigate the effects of patterns of land use, pollution levels, the uptake of eco-friendly technology, and access to digital technologies on reducing poverty in rural areas. The dataset combines characteristics related to poverty alleviation, such as average income levels and access to essential services, with measurements of land use, pollution levels, eco-technology adoption rates, rural industrial output/growth rates, and access to digital technologies. Spatial autocorrelation was assessed using the global Moran index, and the findings show significant spatial clustering in areas with comparable ecological strategies as a result of the findings, which demonstrated the existence of spatial autocorrelation, spatial lag, or spatial error factors had to be included in the models. The results show that adopting eco-friendly technology and the availability of digital technologies benefit poverty reduction, whereas higher pollution levels have a negative impact. The study also discovered spatial spillover effects, which indicate that changes in one location can significantly impact. The study makes essential policy recommendations, such as promoting environmentally friendly technologies, improving digital infrastructure, enforcing environmental regulations, and targeting interventions considering spatial factors. The study is constrained by factors like possible biases in the data sources, built-in restrictions in the spatial econometric models, and limitations on generalizability. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: China; Ecological strategies; Green digital technology; Moran index; Poverty alleviation; Rural industrial development; Spatial patterns

Authors: Hu Y.; Huang G.; Yang Y.
Title: Brand marketing strategy with social responsibility: product line design and pricing
Abstract: We study the brand marketing with social responsibility (BMSR) strategy in a cooperative-platform supply chain with two types of customers heterogeneous in product valuation and prosocial preference. Under the BMSR strategy, some regular products are promoted as social responsibility products, which generate warm glow effects on the prosocial customers. For the centralized system, we consider with and without the BMSR strategies. For the decentralized system, we consider without the BMSR strategy, cooperative-led (platform-led) BMSR strategy wherein the cooperative (platform) implements the BMSR. We find that when the prosocial customers are high-end customers, the BMSR strategy should be always launched. However, if the prosocial customers are low-end customers, the BMSR strategy is effective only when cost coefficient of the brand marketing and unit production cost are small enough. Moreover, we find that the BMSR strategy can mitigate customer cannibalization, and the entire customer surplus can be fully extracted under certain conditions. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Brand marketing; E-commerce supply chain; Pricing; Product line design; Social responsibility

Authors: Ye L.; Cho J.; Bian Y.
Title: Decoding signals: the impact of digital media exposure on K-pop firm performance
Abstract: Digital media news has emerged as a transformative force propelling businesses of K-pop firms onto the global arena. Yet, our understanding of how digital media exposure affects K-pop firm performance remains limited. Drawing on the magic bullet theory, this study explores and sheds light on the performance implications of digital media exposure of firms competing in the K-pop industry. Using an extensive panel dataset spanning twenty-four years, our investigation reveals that digital media exposure is positively associated with K-pop firm performance. Importantly, the nature of this impact is intricately tied to the specific narrative styles employed in the digital media discourse. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Digital media exposure; Firm performance; Narrative style; The K-pop industry

Authors: Gao S.; Yang Y.
Title: Toward a benchmark for CTR prediction in online advertising: datasets, evaluation protocols and perspectives
Abstract: Click-through rate (CTR) prediction aims to infer the probability that users will click on advertisements, which is crucial for platforms to plan and optimize the expected revenues in online advertising. Substantial research efforts have been invested to develop a variety of CTR prediction models. However, there remains a lack of standardized benchmarks for validating and comparing the performance of CTR prediction models, especially in the situation with numerous models developed by researchers and practitioners. This research designs a unified architecture of CTR prediction benchmark (Bench-CTR) platform that offers flexible interfaces with datasets and components of a wide range of CTR prediction models. Moreover, we construct a comprehensive system of evaluation protocols encompassing real-world and synthetic datasets, a taxonomy of metrics, standardized procedures and experimental guidelines for calibrating the performance of CTR prediction models. Furthermore, we implement the proposed benchmark platform and conduct a comparative study to evaluate a wide range of state-of-the-art models from traditional multivariate statistical to modern large language model (LLM)-based approaches on three public datasets and two synthetic datasets. Experimental results reveal that, (1) high-order models largely outperform low-order models, though such advantage varies in terms of metrics and on different datasets; (2) LLM-based models demonstrate a remarkable data efficiency, i.e., achieving the comparable performance to other models while using only 2% of the training data; (3) the performance of CTR prediction models has achieved significant improvements from 2015 to 2016, then reached a stage with slow progress, which is consistent across various datasets. This benchmark is expected to facilitate model development and evaluation and enhance practitioners’ understanding of the underlying mechanisms of models in the area of CTR prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/NuriaNinja/Bench-CTR. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Benchmarking framework; CTR prediction; Evaluation protocol; Online advertising

Authors: Dong X.; Li X.; Yao T.
Title: How configuration strategy of multi-subject generated signals influences online consumption decision-making: the FsQCA investigation based on signaling theory
Abstract: Existing studies have mainly focused on the influence of a single signaling subject on consumer decision-making, while ignoring the comprehensive influence mechanism of multi-subject generated signals on consumer decision-making on bilateral platforms. To fill this research gap, this study draws on signaling theory to develop a framework that illustrates how multi-subject generated signals from resource suppliers, resource demanders and platforms affect consumer shopping decisions. Based on the transaction data from 5,317 consumers on an e-commerce platform, we conduct three studies and utilize Fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) to examine our proposed framework and explore what configurations of multi-subject generated signals may be present to facilitate high levels of consumer browsing and purchasing behaviors. Our findings have important practical implications for both e-commerce platforms and consumers. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Browsing decision; Configuration analysis; FsQCA; Purchase decision; Signaling theory

Authors: Chen Y.; Liu L.; Zhou M.; Liu H.
Title: Effects of construal matching on repurchase behavior: evidence from a field experiment
Abstract: Effectively delivering promotional SMS is crucial in competitive markets—particularly in the airline industry. This study examines the matching effect between promotional messages (high- vs. low-level construal) and purchase probability (low vs. high) on repurchase behavior. Using a large-scale field experiment conducted during the “Double 12” event run by an airline in China, we find that matching low purchase probability with high-level construal has a significant negative effect on consumer repurchase behavior. By contrast, matching high purchase probability with low-level construal has a significant positive effect. These findings provide theoretical guidance and practical implications for precision marketing, customized advertising, and consumer service strategies. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Construal level theory; Field experiment; Probabilistic distance; Promotional message; Repurchase behavior

Authors: Duan K.; Wang Z.; Zhang X.; Zhao H.; Liu Y.
Title: Deeds not words: the impact of past satisfaction and behavior on cross-buying intention on online course platforms
Abstract: The emergence of online course platforms (OCPs) provides a convenient channel for individuals to acquire knowledge, and also attracts learners to pay for knowledge. For these platforms, how to attract new learners as well as improve learner’s retention is an important issue. The purpose of this study is to analyze the mechanism by which learners’ satisfaction with the platform and learning behaviors affect their intention to knowledge cross-buying. In addition, we examine the moderating effect of knowledge product characteristics. To address these issues, we estimate the influence of learners’ satisfaction and learning behavior on knowledge cross-buying intention based on the probit regression method. The authors collected data from two online medical course platforms operated by a Chinese company. The dataset includes course information, learner’s behavior and login data of 3143 learners from January 2014 to December 2019. Knowledge cross-buying behavior occurs between this new platform and another existing platform. The results reveal that learners’ satisfaction and behavior with conviction positively impact knowledge cross-buying intention. Conversely, behavior without conviction does not have a significant impact, which may be attributed to path dependence. Furthermore, the effects of behavior with conviction are amplified when the courses are more difficult, while the effects of learners’ satisfaction are comparatively weaker. By examining the influence of OCPs learners’ learning environment and their learning behavior on the intention to pay for knowledge, as well as the moderating effect of knowledge product characteristics, this study explains the mechanism behind learners' payment for knowledge. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Knowledge cross-buying; Knowledge payment; Loyalty program; Online course platforms

Authors: Sun Y.; Qiu R.; Shao S.; Sun M.
Title: Omnichannel strategies and pricing and ordering decisions of an online-first retailer considering product returns
Abstract: This study examines the deployment of two mainstream omnichannel strategies, i.e., “experience in store and buy online” (ESBO) and “buy online and return in store” (BORS), for an online-first retailer. The retailer charges a fee for online returns but offers a full refund for in-store returns. The expected profit maximization models for the online-only and the omnichannel strategies are developed and the optimal results are analyzed. The optimal pricing and ordering decisions are examined and the conditions for the retailer to benefit from deploying the ESBO or BORS strategy are explored. The main findings provide guidelines for retailer to operate physical stores or showrooms. The retailer can benefit from operating a physical store if the customer online return penalty is low and the product differentiation degree is high, if the product has low valuation and moderate differentiation degree, if the product has high valuation and high differentiation degree, if the reverse logistics cost is high, or if the product differentiation degree is moderate and the customer travel cost is low. The retailer can benefit from operating a showroom if the product has low valuation and high differentiation degree, if the online return penalty and the product differentiation degree are high, if the reverse logistics cost is moderate, or if the product differentiation degree and the customer travel cost are both moderate. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Customer returns; Omnichannel strategies; Ordering decision; Partial refund; Pricing

Authors: Lu Y.; Liu Y.; Zhao G.
Title: Influences of online reviews and in-sale services on a dual-channel supply chain
Abstract: There exist a few channel conflicts and online reviews and offline in-sale services deeply affect the shopping behavior of consumers and the profits of supply chain. To well analyse the influences of online reviews and offline in-sale services on supply chain and deal with channel conflicts, we establish some basic models involving online reviews and in-sale services based on market demand theory and Stackelberg game theory, and exploit them to discuss the optimal decisions under the different decision. And then, we construct a two-part tariff contract. Finally, a number case verifies the validity of the proposed model. The results show that (1) Appropriate investment in online reviews and in-sale services can enhance the overall profits of the supply chain. (2) Compared with in-sale services, online reviews have a more significant impact on supply chain pricing strategies. (3) The introduced coordination mechanism can effectively resolve channel conflicts by reducing wholesale prices and introducing transfer fees. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Channel conflicts; In-sale services; Online reviews; Transfer fee; Two-part tariff

Authors: Liu J.; Ma Z.
Title: The role of multiple online information sources in enhancing physicians’ healthcare quality
Abstract: This paper studies how multiple information, namely, physician-generated information and other-generated information (online reviews and consultation volume), jointly affect online healthcare quality enhancement decisions. We build a two-period service enhancement model consisting of high- and low-quality physicians and consumers who are unsure of the healthcare quality. Additionally, consultation volume is random because some healthcare platforms offer rapid medical consultation services using algorithms to randomly match physicians with random consumers. We find that: (i) contrary to the wisdom that more information can improve healthcare enhancement investment and profits, the incentive impact of information disclosure also depends on several factors, including the scale of random consultation volume, the magnitude of the enhancement cost and how consumers infer the quality difference from physician-generated information; (ii) the accuracy of online reviews and consultation volume influence the online registered physicians’ strategies. Such findings offer guidelines for designing effective information disclosure policies in the online healthcare market. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Consultation volume; Healthcare quality enhancement; Online review; Other-generated information; Physician-generated information; Signaling theory

Authors: Qin C.; Hu C.; Feng Y.
Title: A novel bidding strategy based on dynamic targeting in real-time bidding market
Abstract: Driven by big data analytics technology, real-time bidding (RTB) advertising has emerged as a mainstream paradigm in online advertising. Demand-side platform, acting as advertiser agents, develop bidding strategy for advertiser to match target audience and to make appropriate bid price in RTB market. Most bidding strategies target potential audience via cookie-based data analysis and market segmentation. However, due to the granularity choice dilemma, segmenting market in advance according to historical data may lead to targeting deviation. In addition, it cannot capture the changes of users’ interests in time. A novel bidding strategy with dynamic targeting is proposed in this study. A user similarity model is constructed by calculating the distance between reaching user and typical target audience in a feature space. On this basis, bid price is determined. The profile of target audience is depicted based on prior information and then adjusted dynamically according to users’ responses. Hence, targeting deviation caused by market segmentation which relies heavily on historical data is reduced. Additionally, we theoretically reveal a negative correlation between bid price and revenue along with a positive correlation between bid price and impression rate. Computational experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our strategy over the existing strategy in this regard. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2023.
Author Keywords: Bidding strategy; Real-time bidding; Target audience; User similarity

Authors: Trabelsi N.; Abakah E.J.A.; Yadav M.P.; Tiwari A.K.
Title: Causal dynamics among fintech, communication and digital payments firms
Abstract: Financial technology (fintech), communication, and digital payments have significantly contributed to the evolution of e-commerce. Inspired by the rapidly growing innovations in e-commerce models induced mainly by fintech and digital payment firms, this study examines the time-varying causality and linkages between fintech stocks (KBW Nasdaq Financial Technology Index) and firms in future payments and future communication businesses (S&P Kensho Future Payments Index and S&P Kensho Future Communication Index) using a battery of three robust estimation techniques with daily data from May 15, 2017, to May 7, 2022. To this end, the rolling window wavelet correlation results demonstrate the existence of positive market correlation at short and medium horizons, mainly between fintech and future payment firms, and a variety of causal relationships, with a notable decrease during the COVID-19 crisis due to the decrease in e-commerce. However, Granger causality between the markets was noticed at different market periods and frequencies. Additionally, the influence of phase synchronization and frequency specificity in the causation between analyzed assets also supports the positive relationship among analyzed assets, implying power predictability among them. These findings have managerial, theoretical, and policy implications, especially in terms of the improved implementation of these new technologies in firms' financial structures. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Causality; Co-movement; Digital payments; Fintech; Traditional assets

Authors: Hettler F.M.; Hammer J.; Teuteberg F.
Title: Unraveling user perception of peer-to-peer lending platforms: a triangulated mixed-methods approach
Abstract: This study investigates user perceptions of European peer-to-peer lending platforms through a post-positivist lens, guided by abductive reasoning. We aimed to explore user themes expressed in reviews, quantify user preferences, and interpret the underlying social constructs shaping platform engagement. Drawing on 2,480 user reviews from 66 platforms (2012–2023), we used natural language processing to identify key concerns and affective responses. A choice-based conjoint experiment (n = 197) modeled how users evaluate trade-offs between five platform attributes. To deepen these findings, we conducted a qualitative analysis of user forum discussions, capturing how trust, risk perception, motivation, and social influence guide user behavior. By triangulating computational, experimental, and interpretive approaches, we provide a multidimensional understanding of user expectations, concerns, and decision-making processes. The study contributes to research on user behavior in digital finance and offers theoretical implications and practical design implications for user-centered peer-to-peer lending services in Europe. © The Author(s) 2025.
Author Keywords: Choice-based conjoint analysis; Mixed-methods; Natural language processing; Peer-to-peer (P2P) lending; Qualitative analysis; User perception

Authors: Ji M.; Liu Y.; Chen X.
Title: The impact of the match between the virtual anchor and brand image on consumers’ purchase intention in virtual live streaming
Abstract: Despite the growing popularity of virtual live streaming, limited research has explored how the alignment between a virtual anchor’s traits and a brand’s image affects consumer outcomes. In this study, we conduct three lab experiments to examine the effect of matching the virtual anchor’s appearance, voice, and language style with a brand’s image on consumer purchase intention. We identify two key mediators—processing fluency and perceived affinity—that explain how this alignment influences purchase intention. Our findings show that both the fit between the virtual anchor’s appearance and the brand image, as well as the fit between the anchor’s voice and the brand image, positively affect consumers’ purchase intention. When it comes to language style, figurative language increases purchase intentions for brands perceived as warm, while literal language does not enhance purchase intentions for brands perceived as competent. Additionally, processing fluency and perceived affinity mediate the relationship between virtual anchor appearance-brand image fit and consumer purchase intention. Specifically, processing fluency mediates the effect of the fit between the anchor’s voice and brand image on purchase intention, while perceived affinity plays a moderating mediating role between the warm and competent brand images. Perceived affinity also mediates the effect of language style fit on purchase intention, while processing fluency does not serve as a mediator in this context. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Brand image; Match-up; Perceived affinity; Processing fluency; Purchase intention; Virtual anchor

Authors: Luo N.; Zhang D.; Zhu Z.; Ni Y.; Cao Q.
Title: What drives the choice between first-hand and second-hand commodities?? An FsQCA analysis
Abstract: The choice between purchasing first-hand or second-hand commodities is shaped by a complex interplay of factors, yet existing research often examines these determinants in isolation, overlooking their combined causal effects. Anchored in signal theory, this study employs fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) to uncover how causal configurations of brand value (BV), commodity price (CP), store reputation (SR), total comment volume (TC), and picture comment volume (PC) collectively influence consumers’ purchasing decisions. Analyzing data from 75 sampled cases (45 first-hand commodities and 30 s-hand commodities), the study reveals three key findings: (1) brand value and store reputation are necessary to achieve high monthly sales of first-hand commodities, while only brand value is a necessary condition for achieving high monthly sales of second-hand commodities; (2) a single dominant configuration (BV*CP*SR*TC*PC) drives high adoption of first-hand commodities; (3) two equifinal pathways (BV*TC*PC vs. BV*~CP*SR*PC) explain the purchasing of second-hand commodities. Theoretically, this research enriches consumer behavior literature by bridging signal theory with a configurational perspective, demonstrating how heterogeneous value perceptions lead to divergent outcomes. Practically, it provides actionable insights for e-commerce platforms: sellers of first-hand commodities should emphasize the synergy among brand, price, and reputation, whereas sellers of second-hand commodities can adopt dual strategies—either leveraging trust signals (reputation and comments) or value-for-money cues (brand and price trade-offs). By highlighting the causal complexity underlying consumer choices, this study advocates for tailored marketing strategies aligned with distinct product contexts. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Causal configurations; Consumers’ purchasing decision; First-hand commodities; Fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis; Second-hand commodities

Authors: Mohd Daud S.N.; Ahmad A.H.; Mohd Sidek N.Z.
Title: Internet finance, institutional quality and economic growth: an empirical insight
Abstract: This paper explores the relationship between internet finance and economic growth. We also construct the internet finance index using four categories and 11 keywords. By employing data from 34 countries from 1996 to 2019, the empirical results indicate that there is a negative effect of internet finance on the country’s economic growth, which was driven by online banking and payment activities. The results suggest that shifting from conventional to online transactions would not involve capital mobilization; instead, it would attract more customers by providing an efficient and safe platform for online transactions. This situation is a point of departure for the enhancement and deepening of internet finance by building customer confidence in financial innovations, stimulating online financing and investment activities, capital mobilization, and thus enhancing economic growth. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Economic growth; Financial innovation; Internet finance index; Technology

Authors: Tian B.; Wang H.
Title: E-coupon or return-freight insurance? Selection of the online retailer’s return-freight compensation policy
Abstract: In real life, return-freight insurance generally fails to effectively identify excessive returns due to consumer impulse consumption. Taking this into account, we introduce an e-coupon policy to reimburse returned consumers for return-freight fees and focus on the online retailers’ choices of return-freight compensation policies. We develop a novel model for consumer returns with e-coupons to compensate for the return-freight fees, and analyze the optimal selection of return-freight compensation policies for the online retailers. Results show that the e-coupon policy can be an effective alternative to free return-freight insurance policy, especially for retailers facing high insurance premiums. Furthermore, both the return rate and discount rate play a critical role in the application of the e-coupon policy, and retailers can expand the potential of the e-coupon policy by improving the discount rate at a moderate return rate. Finally, we suggest that online retailers can choose an appropriate return-freight compensation policy based on their own return situation and grasp of the return policy. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: E-commerce; Insurance strategy; Price adjustment protection policy; Strategic consumers

Authors: Yang Z.; Pan R.
Title: Product service supply chain decision considering network externality and corporate social responsibility in digital servitization
Abstract: In digital servitization, manufacturers develop three mature servitization models, namely, service self-operating, service outsourcing, and cooperative service provision, which allows for the formation of the product service supply chain (PSSC) with retailers and customers. This study constructs a three-echelon PSSC considering the network externality and the corporate social responsibility (CSR) based on the Stackelberg game. The optimal solutions under centralized and decentralized decisions are given, and comparative analyses among different models are presented. The research objective of this paper is to clarify the contribution of network externality and CSR to the value creation of stakeholders in the process of digital servitization. The results show that network externality always increases the service price, service level, profit, and the product and service CSR levels. The concern of manufacturers and retailers for product and service CSR investments influences the decision of PSSC. In the cooperative service provision model, the increased customer numbers can directly improve the service price, service level and service CSR level, but the overall benefit is less than the model where manufacturers conduct service self-operating or outsourcing. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Corporate social responsibility; Digital servitization; Network externality; Product service supply chain; Servitization model

Authors: Liao S.-H.; Widowati R.; Linh P.N.M.
Title: Delivery service and omni-channel online-and-offline for retail collaborative recommendations
Abstract: The delivery service business model is the final link in logistics for both online-and-offline business. An omni-channel, the online-and-offline business model, provides a complete and uninterrupted consumption experience by combining data and marketing content, acting as an offline physical channel and online with consistent services and information. Online-and-offline business models combine e-commerce and physical commerce. Regarding retail collaborative recommendation, that is, a recommendation mechanism involves two or more parties, such as logistics, retail firms and e-commerce operators, working together to obtaining necessary consumer information and knowledge, such as profiles and preferences, as the basis for personalized product recommendations. Thus, this study investigates Vietnamese consumer behaviors with delivery services and omni-channel online-and-offline (n = 2354). Data mining analytics, including clustering analysis and association rules, reveal knowledge clusters/patterns/rules for investigating delivery service and omni-channel online-and-offline for retail collaborative recommendations. Finally, discussion and implications are presented. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Data mining analytics; Delivery service; Omni-channel; Online-and-offline; Retail collaborative recommendations

Authors: Ding Z.; Cui Z.; Huang W.
Title: Implications of consumer manipulation and inequity aversion on transparency of personalized pricing
Abstract: The increasingly mature information technology and abundant data resources are empowering platform companies to employ personalized pricing strategies by using consumer privacy information. However, real-world cases introduce additional complexities, as consumers not only consider transnational fairness but also consider concealing their identities to protect their private information. Surprisingly, little research has explicitly examined the question of whether platform companies should disclose their personalized pricing practices in the presence of consider consumer fairness with privacy preferences concerns. In this paper, we present a game-theoretic model that addresses this gap by exploring a dynamic market environment where platform companies make strategic decisions on transparent personalized pricing strategies. Contrary to conventional wisdom, this study findings reveal that personalized pricing transparency can sometimes yield increased profits for platform companies but simultaneously diminish consumer surplus and social welfare. Consequently, from public policy makers perspective, mandating platform companies to disclose the collection and utilization of consumer data may inadvertently harm consumer interests and result in unintended consequences. Moreover, we ascertain that when anonymity costs are moderate, company profits exceed those achieved under the company’s transparent personalized pricing strategy when consumer fairness is considered, but as anonymity costs increase, consumer surplus and social welfare tend to decrease. Therefore, while public policy makers advocate for company’s transparent personalized pricing strategy, this study research suggests that platform companies should carefully evaluate decisions on transparent personalized pricing strategies, taking into account consumers’ anonymity cost and concerns regarding fairness. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Consumer privacy; Fairness concerns; Personalized pricing; Social welfare

Authors: Cao P.; Peng Y.; Liang S.; Su Z.
Title: Privacy breaches and ambiguity aversion: investigating the impact on e-commerce decision making
Abstract: In the era of big data, consumer privacy decision-making, as a complex, dynamic, and increasingly important research area within the context of e-commerce, has garnered significant attention from various sectors. While much of the existing literature focuses on privacy breaches and deterministic behaviors (i.e., decision-making based on clear and known information), less attention has been paid to uncertain behaviors, such as ambiguity aversion (i.e., the preference for known risks over unknown probabilities). This study aims to fill this gap by exploring the impact of privacy breaches on consumers’ ambiguity aversion behaviors and the underlying mechanisms. The results of three experiments indicate that consumers exhibit significantly stronger ambiguity aversion when facing privacy breaches (compared to no breaches), with privacy concerns mediating this effect. Additionally, information sensitivity (high vs. low) moderates this relationship. Specifically, for consumers with high information sensitivity, privacy breaches heighten privacy concerns, leading to ambiguity-averse behavior in subsequent purchase decisions. However, this effect is absent among consumers with low information sensitivity. These findings not only enrich research on privacy breaches and ambiguity aversion but also provide new insights into consumer psychology and marketing strategies in the context of e-commerce. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Ambiguity aversion; Information sensitivity; Privacy breach; Privacy concern

Authors: Li C.; Qiu R.; Sun M.
Title: Virtual showroom strategies for online channels with consumer disappointment aversion
Abstract: Consumer product valuation uncertainty may trigger disappointment aversion, and may lead to consumer virtual showrooming, i.e., viewing the products in virtual showrooms online and purchasing the products offline. This study examines the online channel virtual showroom strategies with consumer disappointment aversion in both monopolistic and competitive markets, and inspects consumer incentives for choosing virtual showrooming with the same or different virtual view costs. The findings show that virtual showrooms have the offline retail price increase effect, the disappointment aversion mitigation effect and the consumer screening effect. In a monopolistic market, disappointment aversion induces more consumers to choose virtual showrooming when the virtual view cost is sufficiently low, indicating that the monopolistic retailer can benefit from consumer disappointment by establishing a virtual showroom. However, in a competitive market, either a higher product fitness probability or a lower disappointment aversion level helps the online channel maintain its price advantage, although the screening effect harms the offline retailer as the virtual view cost increases. The competitiveness of offline channels weakens as consumer disappointment aversion level decreases and virtual view cost increases, and the online channels benefit more from the virtual showroom in the competitive market than in the monopolistic market. Virtual showrooms encourage consumers who are sensitive to travel costs to switch from online to offline channels when they all have the same virtual view cost, but may not when they have different virtual view costs. Consumer virtual showrooming intensifies channel competition, and aggressive pricing decisions may negatively impact social welfare. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Consumer disappointment aversion; Consumer virtual showrooming; E-commerce; Market structures; Product valuation uncertainty

Authors: Sun Y.; Ma C.; Han J.; Işık C.
Title: A game theory approach of cross-border e-commerce enterprises from China considering brand promotion
Abstract: This study applies game theory to examine strategies for cross-border e-commerce enterprises considering independent website deployment amidst platform restrictions. We constructed a model to analyze the impact of channel preferences, pricing, and commission rates on the decision to establish an independent online presence in China. Findings reveal that channel choice and sales costs significantly influence this decision, with platform commission rates dictating operational modes—lower rates favor traffic acquisition, while higher rates encourage brand building. Expansion through independent websites emerges as a superior strategy over platform reliance alone. The study offers clear recommendations, suggesting that platform providers should reduce commissions to incentivize independent channels, benefiting both parties. Limitations include model assumptions and the specificity of findings to the Chinese market, offering a balanced view of the research’s scope. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Channel selection; Cross-border e-commerce; Game theory; Independent website; Operation model

Authors: Liu Z.; Tan A.L.R.; Dou Z.; Wang X.
Title: Does economic policy uncertainty promote enterprise technological innovation? Mediating role based on financing constraints and risk-taking behaviour
Abstract: This paper describes the influence of economic policy uncertainty (EPU) on firms’ technological innovation and proposes a theoretical model that includes double mediating effects of firms' financing constraints and risk-taking behaviour. On this basis, this study uses Chinese A-share listed businesses from 2012 to 2021 as samples to conduct empirical research on the influence of EPU on technological innovation; a panel data regression model is developed to examine the impact of EPU on enterprise technological innovation empirically. It is found that EPU markedly promotes enterprise technological innovation, and the effect of this promotion is moderated by the form of ownership and its concentration. Further research shows that financing constraints negatively and partly mediated the effect of EPU on enterprise technological innovation. In contrast, enterprise risk-taking plays a positive and partial mediating role. The conclusion of this study is a certain inspiration and reference for enterprises to rationally understand the influence of EPU, improve corporate governance structure and incentive mechanism, and promote enterprise technology innovation investment decisions in the environment of EPU. Meanwhile, it offers a new perspective to explain the lack of impetus for enterprise technological innovation investment under EPU and provides a theoretical and policy basis for the government to create a good environment to encourage enterprises to invest in technological innovation, alleviate enterprise financing constraints, guide enterprises’ expectations of macroeconomic policies, and stimulate the vitality of enterprise technological innovation. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: EPU; Financing constraints; Risk-taking; Technological innovation

Authors: Xu X.; Wang Y.
Title: Unveil the ignored prewriting stage: linking the efforts input in the prewriting stage with how consumers write their online reviews
Abstract: Online consumer reviews can help businesses understand consumers’ needs and wants, generate electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM) to increase sales, and thus have significant business value. Consumers have emotional, psychological, social, and altruistic motivations to write and post online reviews. However, writing online reviews requires effort and incurs time and hassle. The effort does not start from the actual writing moment but begins with consumers’ intention to write online reviews after consumption. This study links the consumers’ effort input in the prewriting stage with how consumers write their online reviews, namely, the linguistic characteristics of reviews. How consumers write their reviews determines the attractiveness and trustworthiness perceived by peer consumers and thus can arouse their empathy and resonance, calling for the importance of studying this important topic. Our empirical findings suggest that a higher prewriting effort reduces review length and readability but increases review clout, tone, authority, and analytical thinking. In addition, the prewriting effort positively affects consumer overall ratings. The interactions of various prewriting efforts have a positive moderating effect on online review linguistic styles and ratings. Our findings offer important managerial implications for businesses to optimally design their review website to affect consumers’ information search process and the associated time and hassle costs to input the appropriate effort to write reviews with a favorable linguistic style. Businesses thus can better utilize online reviews with favorable linguistic features to enhance the eWOM effect to attract potential consumers and improve sales and financial performance. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: E-commerce; Linguistic characteristics; Online consumer reviews; Prewriting effort

Authors: Wong A.
Title: The social fabric of brand communities: how cognitive and relational social capital shape commitment and advocacy
Abstract: Integrating the Social Capital Theory, Social Identity Theory, and Social Exchange Theory, this study explores how the cognitive and relational dimensions of social capital affect brand community commitment and brand community advocacy in peer-initiated social media brand communities (SMBCs). Data was collected from an online survey of 917 active brand community members. This study focuses on peer-initiated SMBCs as opposed to company-initiated SMBCs due to the process in which social capital shapes the organic and voluntary nature of consumer participation in peer-initiated SMBCs. The results reveal that shared vision and reciprocity influence brand community commitment, positivity, and defense. In addition, brand community commitment mediates the relationships between the social capital dimensions and both brand positivity and brand defense. By integrating the direct and indirect effects of social capital on brand advocacy mechanisms, the findings extend existing research on social media marketing and offer brand managers valuable insights into cultivating brand advocacy behaviors within peer-initiated SMBCs. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Brand advocacy; Brand commitment; Social capital; Social media brand communities

Authors: Xiao X.; Xia Y.; Xiang Z.; Hu Y.
Title: Village e-commerce enhances rural household consumption diversity: evidence from rural China
Abstract: Consumption diversity in rural areas of developing countries remains relatively low, and e-commerce penetration in villages offers a potential solution to address this challenge. Employing China’s National Rural E-commerce Comprehensive Demonstration Policy as a quasi-natural experiment and using a staggered difference-in-differences method alongside household-level survey data from China for the years 2011 to 2019, this study examines the impact of e-commerce penetration in villages on rural household consumption diversity and its underlying mechanisms. The findings indicate that e-commerce penetration significantly increases the consumption diversity index of rural households by 0.01, a result that remains robust across various checks. Increasing agricultural income, enhancing digital literacy and improving rural logistics and communications infrastructure are the three key mechanisms through which e-commerce penetration enhances consumption diversity among rural households. Heterogeneity analysis reveals that e-commerce penetration primarily enhances consumption diversity among low-endowment rural households. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Consumption diversity; E-commerce penetration in villages; Rural China; Staggered DID

Authors: Wang X.; Zhang Y.; Li H.; Zuo Z.
Title: Treat or quit: churn prediction in online health communities based on inverse reinforcement learning
Abstract: User churn in OHCs have hence garnered widespread academic attention for a better understanding of engagement patterns, which, in turn, facilitates better platform design and management. This study applies inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to capture intrinsic drivers shaping user engagement, which offers a novel predictive framework on churn. Based on a dataset of 1081 users from a breast cancer OHC, we implemented an IRL model whose states refer to user reception of informational support, emotional support, and companionship and actions refer to engagement behaviors in the form of thread initiation, replying, and self-replies. The IRL-based reward values, which reflect intrinsic drivers of user engagement, improve churn prediction accuracy across short-, mid-, and long-term time frames with F1 scores improving by up to 19.85%. Our findings contribute to the literature on user churn and retention in OHCs, and offer managerial implications for platform managers. © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025.
Author Keywords: Inverse reinforcement learning; Online health community; User behavior analysis; User churn prediction

Authors: Xu J.; Xi W.; Shen H.
Title: Dynamic scheduling between testers and tasks in crowdtesting
Abstract: Crowdtesting, an emerging practice in software testing, relies on crowd testers to execute test assignments.
